title: From Robotaxis To Humanoids: Why Embodied AI Represents An Order-Of-Magnitude Leap Into The Future

From Robotaxis To Humanoids: Why Embodied AI Represents An Order-Of-Magnitude Leap Into The Future

Mentioned Companies:
TSLA

From Robotaxis To Humanoids: Why Embodied AI Represents An Order-Of-Magnitude Leap Into The Future

July 01, 2026
12 min read

By: Akaash TK

Research Associate, Autonomous Technology & Robotics

More from Akaash TK

Join the conversation

Akaash TK joined ARK in July 2024. As a Research Associate, he is responsible for aiding investment decisions by analyzing and evaluating opportunities in the Autonomous Technology and Robotics sector, focusing on innovations that drive significant market impact and value growth.

Prior to ARK, Akaash pursued a management degree at New York University, where he specialized in financial modeling and valuation analysis, assessing the impact of new technologies on company valuations. During his undergraduate studies in India, he worked with a space startup to build the world’s first fully 3D printed rocket engine and led several additive manufacturing projects.

Akaash holds a Bachelor's degree in Mechanical Engineering from VIT University, and a Master's degree in Management of Technology from New York University.

The Potential Of Humanoid RobotsToday’s humanoid robot capability certainly seems limited relative to its potential. According to our research, humanoid systems could prove transformational for the modern workforce as they expand automation into labor-constrained sectors such as logistics, manufacturing, healthcare, and services. In 2028, humanoids could reach a level of operational performance comparable to that of today’s robotaxis.As they scale, contrary to prevailing public fears, humanoid systems are likely to be net job creators. Historically, major technological breakthroughs have raised labor productivity by automating routine tasks and enabling workers to focus on higher-value work.Indeed, productivity accelerated following the adoption of new technologies like tractors, assembly lines, computers, and the internet, as shown below—all innovations that faced labor displacement fears at the time. In each case, those fears proved misplaced as technology ultimately expanded output and opportunity. Since the commercial tractor’s debut, US labor productivity has compounded at ~2.1%1 annually, and real disposable income has grown at a 3.2%2 annualized rate over the postwar period. Little evidence indicates that generalizable robots will break from that pattern. Our research suggests that both productivity and real disposable income will accelerate meaningfully from their recent first-quarter year-over-year growth of 2.8%3 and 0.7%,4 respectively, over the next five to ten years.Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026, based on data from U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics and U.S. Census Bureau as of June 17, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.Continued advances are necessary for generalizable robots to realize their potential. Today’s deployments are confined largely to structured environments and narrowly defined tasks, such as pick-and-place operations with human oversight in warehouses and factory floors. While companies like Agility Robotics and Boston Dynamics have demonstrated impressive performance within these constraints, true autonomy would require robots that can navigate real-world environments to execute complex workflows, like operating an entire assembly line or factory, without human intervention.The Complexity Of Generalized RoboticsA generalizable robot represents one of the most complex engineering challenges in autonomy. According to our research, a humanoid robot is 200,000 times more complex than a robotaxi. We measure complexity across five dimensions: kinetic demand, dynamic mobility, perception and reasoning, adaptability, and error tolerance, as shown below.Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026. The chart presents results from ARK’s internal analyses, which draw on a range of various sources of data, including company reports as of June 17, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.A robotaxi operates within a relatively constrained set of governing controls, primarily steering, acceleration, and braking. In contrast, a generalizable robot must coordinate an order of magnitude more joints that simultaneously control motion across multiple limbs. Unlike optimizing a single drivetrain, these systems must manage dozens of finely tuned robotic actions at the same time.While highly sophisticated, a robotaxi’s perception and reasoning systems operate in a fairly structured environment with defined rules, common obstacles, and a single objective: moving from Point A to Point B safely. Generalizable robots, by comparison, must function across highly unstructured settings, from factory floors to homes and gardens, navigating uneven and unpredictable surfaces while executing a wide range of tasks that extend well beyond locomotion.Error tolerance is one factor that we believe works in favor of generalizable robots. Operating at human-scale speed and kinetic energy, a misstep is unlikely to be catastrophic. For context, robotaxis are ~20 times heavier and operate at speeds much higher than humans, suggesting that errors can cause serious human and other casualties. This distinction impacts both system design and deployment risk.To reach the threshold of complexity at which robotic performance approximates human-level competence, software and hardware must advance significantly. The software challenge extends well beyond model architecture to the acquisition of physical world data. Unlike the relatively narrow distribution of tasks and environments that an autonomous vehicle must surmount, generalizable robots must learn from highly heterogeneous interactions with the physical world.Collecting that data is a slow, expensive, and failure-prone process, as each data point links the robot to a physical task, often involving edge cases difficult to reproduce or label. Evidence that these constraints represent serious challenges are the limited number of generalizable robots in operation globally today, as shown below.Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026. The chart presents results from ARK’s internal analyses, which draw on a range of various sources of information, including data from the International Federation of Robotics and numerous company reports as of June 17, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.FANUC, KUKA, and Teradyne, among other companies, have deployed industrial robot platforms at scale for decades: cumulatively, industrial robot unit sales have surpassed 7 million globally. In comparison, quadruped and humanoid robots are in the tens of thousands. In other words, humanoids have had little real-world experience from which to learn.Early beta humanoid deployments are addressing that constraint by scaling the collection of real-world data now that companies like 1X and Sunday Robotics have plans to place systems in customer environments. China is pushing the rapid deployment of robots in real world environments, leveraging lower labor costs and access to its plethora of local supply chains to accelerate data generation and iterate improvements. Chinese companies currently account for almost all humanoid robot sales today, as shown below, reflecting their aggressive push to scale.Note: “RoW” = “Rest of World.” Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026. The chart presents results from ARK’s internal analyses, which draw on a range of various sources of data, including company reports as of June 17, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.Humanoid Learning And ComputeThe scaling of humanoids also faces a second-order challenge: extracting useful learning from physical interactions that are noisy, inconsistent, and often incomplete. The accumulation of real-world robot data is beginning to bootstrap increasingly rich synthetic datasets. That said, generating, validating, and training on these simulations requires substantial compute, as well as careful alignment with real-world physics. The result, we believe, is a software stack that simultaneously must solve for perception, control, reasoning, and continual learning in environments that change constantly and generate many unanticipated edge cases.Likely critical to hardware development will be improvements in sensors, actuators, energy density, and on-device compute. If the performance of each hardware component continues to advance, the gains should compound, not only reducing costs and increasing reliability, but also expanding the operational envelope of generalizable robots.Building on this framework, ARK uses Tesla’s robotaxi performance gains as a scaling reference by mapping improvements in autonomy to deployed compute capacity, as shown below. Our research suggests that Tesla’s Optimus could approach human-level task proficiency around 2028. To hit that level, Tesla will have to invest ~$50 billion for one gigawatt of operational compute and also scale its collection of diverse, real-world data while improving the efficiency of software and hardware consistently.*Note: “MW” = “Megawatts.” *Cumulative AI Compute days benchmarked to H100s at its launch. Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026. The chart presents results from ARK’s internal analyses, which draw on a range of various sources of data, including company reports as of June 17, 2026. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Forecasts are inherently limited and cannot be relied upon.Past performance is not indicative of future results.Progress toward generalizable robotics, or embodied AI, is likely to be uneven and incremental—not linear. While these systems are materially more complex than today’s autonomous platforms, sustained advances in compute architecture, data collection, and simulation should narrow the gap between current autonomous capabilities and general-purpose capability. As real-world deployments scale, improvements are likely to become increasingly visible, though varied by task and environment.If current investment levels and scaling laws for software and hardware improvements persist, generalizable robots could begin to approach human-level task proficiency later this decade. In ARK’s view, physical AI is more likely to augment human labor than replace it outright, enabling accelerated and perhaps surprising productivity growth.

1

U.S. Census Bureau. 1957. “Productivity and Technological Development. Pre-1947. Chapter W.”

2

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED). 2026. “Real Disposable Personal Income (DPIC96) 01-01-1947 – 01-01- 2026.”

3

Based on data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.

4

Based on data from the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis.
